coredata.dc:title: 0D-3D-1D nanoarchitectured CQDs modified NiFe layered double hydroxides supported with MWCNTs: Enhanced electrocatalytic performance for oxygen evolution reaction

coredata.dc:description:
Nickel–Iron Layered Double Hydroxides (NiFe LDH)-based electrocatalysts have evinced tremendous interest towards oxygen evolution reaction (OER) to overcome sluggish water oxidation kinetics. This study reports (i) engineering coordination environment of NiFe LDH by varying Ni–Fe ratios (3:1 to 1:3) and (ii) heterostructuring by growing the optimal 1:1 NiFe LDH in situ on multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT)-modified Ni foam via urea-assisted hydrothermal method and decorating with carbon quantum dots (CQD). The hybrid 0D-CQD/3D-NFL/1D-MWCNT shows unique 3D urchin-morphology comprising 2D nanoribbons with an electrochemical surface area of 252.72 cm2/cm2 geometric area and exhibits best overpotential of 172 mV at 100 mAcm−2, Tafel slope (37.2 mVdec−1), and stability (1.44V vs. RHE for 100 h at 100 mAcm−2). Effectively transferring electrons
O H
−
→
t h r o u g h
C Q D
N i F e
L D H
→ M W C N T → N i
F o a m
, the heterostructured CQD/NFL/CNT exhibits minimal change in overpotentials even at higher current densities (204 mV at 500 mAcm−2 and 212 mV at 750 mAcm−2), demonstrating its enhanced OER properties.

head.ce:title: 0D-3D-1D nanoarchitectured CQDs modified NiFe layered double hydroxides supported with MWCNTs: Enhanced electrocatalytic performance for oxygen evolution reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Nickel–Iron Layered Double Hydroxides (NiFe LDH)-based electrocatalysts have evinced tremendous interest towards oxygen evolution reaction (OER) to overcome sluggish water oxidation kinetics. This study reports (i) engineering coordination environment of NiFe LDH by varying Ni–Fe ratios (3:1 to 1:3) and (ii) heterostructuring by growing the optimal 1:1 NiFe LDH in situ on multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT)-modified Ni foam via urea-assisted hydrothermal method and decorating with carbon quantum dots (CQD). The hybrid 0D-CQD/3D-NFL/1D-MWCNT shows unique 3D urchin-morphology comprising 2D nanoribbons with an electrochemical surface area of 252.72 cm2/cm2 geometric area and exhibits best overpotential of 172 mV at 100 mAcm−2, Tafel slope (37.2 mVdec−1), and stability (1.44V vs. RHE for 100 h at 100 mAcm−2). Effectively transferring electrons
O H
−
→
t h r o u g h
C Q D
N i F e
L D H
→ M W C N T → N i
F o a m
, the heterostructured CQD/NFL/CNT exhibits minimal change in overpotentials even at higher current densities (204 mV at 500 mAcm−2 and 212 mV at 750 mAcm−2), demonstrating its enhanced OER properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Excessive use of non-renewable fossil fuels has accelerated the emission rate of greenhouse gases, which have amplified global warming effects in the environment [1–3]. Thus, there has been a search for renewable and greener energy sources. Hydrogen, being an ideal energy carrier owing to its very high calorific value and clean combustion products, is considered as an effective solution for the search for such an energy source [4–6]. Electrochemical water splitting can produce hydrogen with high purity and in a greener way [7–9]. Oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is a decisive bottleneck for efficient water splitting and increasing hydrogen production rate because of its sluggish kinetics when compared to its counter-reaction, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [10–13]. Precious metal-based catalysts such as RuO2 and IrO2 show better performances but still demand high overpotentials to facilitate OER at commercially relevant current densities. Further, their scarcity and high costs hinder their wide use in commercial applications [14–16]. Therefore, the development of electrocatalysts that are low-cost, earth-abundant, less hazardous, and yet highly effective in enhancing the rate of OER is a critical need.

Extensive studies have been carried out on earth-abundant materials, especially first row transition metal-based catalysts [17–20]. Among the transition metal-based electrocatalysts, nickel-based ones are well-known. Layered double hydroxides (LDH) are the family of layered anionic materials with excellent features such as flexible structures and chemical versatility [21–24]. LDH electrocatalysts have been reported to be the ones with minimum overpotentials for OER as well as minimum Tafel slopes [25]. Among various LDHs, NiFe LDH (NFL) is the most investigated as an electrocatalyst for OER due to its relatively open layered structure that facilitates the quick diffusion of reactants/products leads to rapid proton and electron transfer processes in the OER reaction (with over 670 publications so far, excluding reviews). However, the poor dispersion and low electrical conductivity of NiFe LDHs remain as drawbacks and reduce their catalytic activity towards OER [21,26,27]. In order to overcome these limitations, researchers have conveniently used methods such as; (a) modulating the compositions and coordination environment of LDH nanosheets [28–31], (b) regulating the electronic structures by defect engineering [32] and doping the active sites with heteroatoms [33–35], and (c) compositing with reduced-dimension nanomaterials [36,37].

Thenuwara et al., incorporated Co into NiFe LDH nanosheets for enhancing OER catalytic activity using coprecipitation and intercalation [38]. By varying the composition of Co with respect to the composition of Ni, they achieved better performance with a lower overpotential of ∼265 mV at 10 mAcm−2 in 1 M KOH. Acharya et al., varied the metallic ratio of Ni to Fe and established that the nanoparticles with equal composition of Ni and Fe is performs better at higher current densities [39]. Hou et al., also studied the effect of Ni/Fe ratio in NiFe LDH towards OER and substantially established the experimental results with aid of density functional theory (DFT) analysis [40]. Modulating the Ni/Fe ratio in the LDH effectively alters the local coordination environment. While both Ni and Fe assume 2+ and 3+ oxidation states, literature seems to suggest that the reversible Ni2+/Ni3+ through the M
→
M − OH
→
M-OOH
→
M − O
→
M cycle has higher electrocatalytic activity than the Fe2+/Fe3+ transitions [25,41]. However, it also appears that the Ni2+/Ni3+ transitions are facilitated and regulated by the Fe2+/Fe3+ sites,
OH −
distribution in the LDH, and the intercalating anions [25,31]. Therefore, for a given NiFe LDH synthesis approach (e.g., choice of anion, synthesis conditions, and technique), it is important to determine the optimum Ni/Fe ratio for fastest OER kinetics.

NiFe LDH has been grown hydrothermally through nucleation in solution as well as in situ on the electrode substrate (e.g., nickel foam). Nucleation in the solution phase promotes random growth and thermal energy itself may serve as the driving force for premature termination of the growth. This often results in higher dispersity of the obtained LDH. On the other hand, promoting hydrothermal growth in situ on an electrode substrate (e.g., Ni foam) leads to heterogenous nucleation on the electrode substrate and directed growth. This can result in anisotropic growth of the LDH forming specific morphologies with significantly reduced dispersity. Further, coating the electrode substrate with 1D nanostructures such as carbon nanotubes can enhance the anisotropy of the obtained LDH. This is due to the already anisotropic structure of CNTs, resulting in them serving as shape-directing agents for the growing LDH. These effects are borne out in the present study.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel nanocarbon materials with unique physical and chemical properties [42–44]. CQDs are having small particle size (∼5 nm), possess electron reservoir properties, the band structure and optical properties, fast electron transfer ability and superior electrical conductivity make more apt material for enhancing the catalytic activities as a composite with an actual catalyst material. Researchers have explored the effect of CQDs with NiFe LDH for OER efficiency [45–47]. Tang et al., used CQDs decorated ultrathin NiFe LDH composites as a highly efficient electrocatalyst for OER. The complex CQD/NiFe LDH exhibited high catalytic activity with an overpotential of 235 mV at 10 mAcm−2 current density in 1 M KOH [48]. Recently Hu et al., developed a one-step ionothermal accompanied thermolysis strategy to synthesize ultrathin NiFe LDH nanosheets with N-doped CQDs with a three-dimensional flower like morphology which exhibited an overpotential of 252 mV at 10 mA cm−2 in 0.1 M KOH [49]. It is evident from the previous studies that the incorporation of CQD effectively promotes the electron transfer kinetics and providing better OER catalytic activity.

On the other hand, notable properties such as high specific surface area, network structures, excellent electron tunneling ability, electrical conductivity, and stability resulted in the exploration of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) for hetero structuring a catalyst material to synthesize a self-supporting interconnected conductive porous network structure [50–53]. Chen et al., demonstrated a room temperature coprecipitation method for synthesizing CNT interconnected amorphous NiFe LDH as an efficient OER catalyst [54]. Li et al., synthesized Cs doped NiFe LDH nanosheets supported on CNT network using a one-pot solvothermal reaction as an electrocatalyst for OER with an overpotential of 240 mV at 10 mAcm−2 in 1 M KOH with excellent durability [55]. In both of the cases, addition of CNT network enhanced the OER performance through improving electron transfer between the
OH −
ions and the current collecting electrode substrate (e.g., Ni foam).

Based on the above analysis, this work considers three specific modifications to the NiFe LDH system to further improve its performance, especially at higher current densities of operation: (i) Modulating Composition: The effect of Ni/Fe ratio ranging from Ni-rich to Fe-rich LDHs on the OER kinetics has been examined systematically, (ii) Heterostructuring: to facilitate better electron transfer from
OH −
ions to the LDH and enhance the rate of OER, the NiFe LDH has been decorated with CQDs, (iii) to effectively transfer the electrons from OER at the LDH to the underlying Ni foam thereby further improve the OER kinetics, the NiFe LDH has been grown insitu on MWCNT-coated Ni foam in a facile, one-step hydrothermal process. Thus, we have explored a novel material design strategy to incorporate both CQDs and CNTs at the same time with NiFe LDH to obtain a 0D-3D-1D nanostructured hierarchical CQD/NFL/CNT composite catalyst for enhancing OER in alkaline water electrolysis. In other words, first NFL/CNT is synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal process followed by a simple dip coating in a CQD solution to obtain a 0D-3D-1D nanostructured sandwich array. Detailed structural and electrochemical investigation of this novel electrocatalytic system has been carried out and is reported here.

Potassium hydroxide (KOH, 99.99%), urea (CH₄N₂O, 99.9%) and citric acid (C₆H₈O₇,99.9%) were procured from Merck. Nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2.6H2O, 99.9%) and iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O, 99.9%) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. MWCNT (Multi-Walled Carbon Nanotubes) with outer diameter 20–40 nm and length of ∼5 μm was purchased from Quantum Materials Corporation, India. Ultrapure Type-1 Millipore water with resistivity
≥
18.2 MΩ was used throughout the experiments. Nickel foam (NF) was purchased from The Electrode Store, India.

Nickel Foam (NF) pieces (3 cm × 1 cm) were treated with 3.0 M hydrochloric acid using ultrasonication for 15 min to remove surface oxides. Further the foam was successively cleaned using ethanol and deionized water.

MWCNTs were acid-functionalized using 3:1 ratio of 3 M H2SO4 and 3 M HNO3 mixture. For this, 0.1 g of MWCNT was added to the above acid solution and refluxed at 85 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the as-settled MWCNT was washed with ethanol and water till the pH of the supernatant solution became neutral and then dried at 80 °C for 12 h.

One-step hydrothermal method was adopted to grow NiFe LDH on Ni foam by in-situ. The Ni/Fe ratio (1:1, 2:1, 3:1, 1:2, 1:3) in the precursor solution was varied with a total molar concentration of 0.05 mol. The precursors of NiCl2.6H2O, FeCl3.6H2O with a fixed Ni/Fe ratio along with 0.1 mol of urea were dissolved into 70 ml Type-1 water to synthesize NiFe LDH. The solution was made homogenous by stirring with a magnetic stirrer at room temperature for 2 h. Then it was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave along with the pretreated NF and heated to 150 °C for 12 h. The obtained LDH-grown foam was washed with deionized water and kept dried at 80 °C for 4 h. The sediments at the bottom of the Teflon cup were also filtered, washed multiple times with ethanol/water mixture and dried in an oven. NiFe LDH modified with CNT was prepared by the same procedure mentioned above by adding 20 mg of acid-functionalized CNT along with the same precursors. The as-prepared samples were used for further characterization.

CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method via a combination of urea and citric acid [56]. In this typical procedure, 6g of urea and citric acid each were dissolved in 60 ml deionized water and stirred continuously for 30 min. This solution was subjected to hydrothermal reaction for 5 h at 180 °C in a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave. It was allowed to cool down to room temperature and the solution was let to stand for 24 h. The as-obtained supernatant solution was separated by centrifugation and it contained CQDs, used for further modification of NiFe LDH and NiFe LDH/CNT.

NFL/NF and NFL/CNT/NF electrodes were prepared as described in the above methodology. These electrodes were modified using CQDs by simple dip coating technique. This allowed the decoration of NiFe LDH surfaces with CQD rather than them getting incorporated into the LDH. The CQDs dip-coated NFL/NF and NFL/CNT/NF electrodes were dried at 80 °C for 4 h. After drying, the prepared CQD/NFL/NF and CQD/NFL/CNT/NF electrodes were used for analysing the electrochemical performance of oxygen evolution reaction.
The details of materials characterization and electrochemical techniques used in this work are mentioned in the supporting information.

Scheme 1 presents the synthesis procedure based on the hydrothermal method to synthesize NiFe LDHs coated Ni foams, with or without MWCNT. As depicted in step 1 of Scheme 1, NiFe LDH with varying Ni/Fe ratios is grown in situ on Ni foams by a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis. When NiFe LDH/MWCNT composite is synthesized, acid-functionalized MWCNT is first dispersed in water, following which the Ni and Fe precursors and urea are added to the dispersion, and a cleaned, bare Ni foam is immersed in the dispersion prior to hydrothermal synthesis. Ni and Fe precursors and urea in deionized water form partially dissolved hydroxides or oxyhydroxides in the precursor solution. Once the hydrothermal conditions are initiated, the coating of MWCNT on Ni foam is much faster than the nucleation and growth of the LDH. Therefore, effectively, MWCNT-coated Ni foam is formed first. At the elevated temperature and pressure, nucleation of the partially dissolved hydroxides occurs on the MWCNT-coated Ni foam resulting in the crystal growth of NiFe LDH. Towards the end of the preferred reaction time, through Ostwald ripening process, 3D urchin-like morphologies comprising 2D nanoribbons of NiFe LDH nanostructure, are formed on MWCNT-coated Ni foam [57].

0D CQDs are synthesized separately using another hydrothermal procedure as depicted in Step 2 of Scheme 1. A simple dip coating in the resulting CQD solution decorates the 0D CQDs on 3D NiFe LDH nanostructures supported on 1D MWCNTs-coated Ni foam prepared in Step 1. Dip coating allows the CQDs to get adsorbed on the surface of NiFe LDH nanostructures rather than get incorporated in the bulk, favoring the formation of 0D-3D-1D nanostructured hierarchical CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst. The overall mechanism of formation of the 0D-3D-1D heterostructured CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst is illustrated stepwise using SEM/TEM images in Scheme S1 (Supporting Information).

The crystalline structures of the prepared NiFe LDH samples were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD). NiFe LDHs with various ratios exhibit similar XRD patterns, as depicted in Fig. S1. Among those variations, the LDH with 1:1 Ni/Fe ratio shows highly crystalline structure and almost all structural planes are present in NiFe LDH with prominent intensities. Diffraction peaks arise at 2θ = 11.38o, 23.14o, 34.42o, 35.58o, 39.06o, 46.24o, 59.94o and 61.3o, which can be indexed to the (003), (006), (012), (009), (015), (018), (110) and (113) planes of the.

NiFe LDH phase which agrees well with standard JCPDS No:40–0215 [40,58]. As the amount ofNi or Fe increases, the relative peak intensities of crystallographic planes are diminished. Some of the minor peaks disappear when the Ni or Fe content increases 3 folds (1:3 and 3:1). Therefore, it is evident that increasing Ni or Fe content causes a significant change in the crystal structure of the NiFe LDH system. LDH formed with Ni/Fe ratio 1:1 shows all the distinct peaks, giving the best crystalline nature to the material. This structure also shows the best electrochemical performance for OER (see below, Fig. 5A and Fig. S4). Hence, NiFe LDH with 1:1 ratio has been considered for further modification with CNT and CQDs.

The diffraction patterns of MWCNT, NFL/CNT and CQD/NFL/CNT are also shown in Fig. 1 A. The diffraction patterns of MWCNT reveals the existence two prominent peaks at 2θ = 26.2o and 44.2o, correlating with the (002) and (100) diffraction peaks of graphitic carbon in the XRD pattern of MWCNT (JCPDS No:20–0781) [59]. These two characteristic peaks are maintained in NFL/CNT and CQD/NFL/CNT with reduced intensity owing to the additional presence of NiFe LDH in the composites the formation of heterostructured nanocomposite.

Raman spectra were analyzed to explore the structure of the bonds in the as-prepared electrocatalysts. The Raman shifts detected for NiFe LDH with varying Ni/Fe ratios are depicted in Fig. S2. NiFe LDH having 1:1 Ni/Fe ratio shows distinct peaks with high intensities in the Raman spectra. The peaks at ∼456 and ∼531 cm−1 could be attributed to the characteristic peaks of M-O-M vibrations of NiFe LDH, and the peak at ∼700 cm−1 could be attributed to the intercalated
CO 3
2 −
ions [60]. It is found that when the Ni/Fe ratio changes either way, increasing the ratio above and below one, the above-mentioned characteristic peaks show distortions and shifts with reduced intensities. Thus, the formation of NiFe LDH is confirmed through Raman analysis. Fig. 1B depicts the Raman peaks for MWCNT, CQD, NFL (1:1), NFL/CNT, and CQD/NFL/CNT samples. For the MWCNT and CQD samples, the peaks at ∼1332, ∼1590 cm−1, and ∼2681 cm−1 in MWCNT correspond to D (defect), G (graphitic), and G′ bands (D overtone) respectively [61]. The D band arises from, (a) disordered graphitic carbon in the boundary region, dangling bonds, oxygen-containing groups from acid functionalization, and vacancies, and (b) amorphous carbon on the surface of the graphitic structure. On the other hand, the in-plane vibration of C–C bond from sp2 orbital of carbon causes the formation of G band showing that the nanotubes are highly graphitized [62]. In the case of NFL/CNT, the D, G, and G′ band intensities of MWCNT are greatly reduced due to the presence of NiFe LDH. Further, the characteristic peaks of NiFe LDH at ∼531 and ∼700 cm−1 broaden significantly with a slight shift towards the left. Both these indicate significant interactions between NiFe LDH and MWCNT, arising from the nucleation and crystal growth of LDH on the CNT. However, in CQD/NFL/CNT the bands related to NiFe LDH seem to be quenched and the D, G, and G′ bands of CQD and MWCNT are enhanced due to the effective incorporation of CQDs on the surface of NFL/CNT. The G’ peak of CQD/NFL/CNT nanocomposite has shifted slightly to a higher wavenumber (∼2689 cm−1). This can be attributed to the significant charge transfer interaction between the NiFe LDH nanostructures and the CQDs and MWCNTs [61].

The ratio intensities of the D and G bands (ID/IG), a measure of structural defects and sp3 hybridized carbon, which in turn gives information about the sidewall functionalization in the sample. Further, the intensity ratios of G′ and G bands (IG’/IG) and those of G′ and D bands (IG’/ID) provide direct insights into the extension of graphitic order in the system and the degree of crystallinity, respectively [62,63]. The relative intensity of D/G bands (ID/IG) is ∼1.02 and ∼1.13 for MWCNTs and CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalysts, respectively. The D/G ratio greater than one indicates that the surface of the MWCNT contains some amount of amorphous carbon and the outer layers of the CNTs are chemically modified, which is achieved by the acid functionalization of MWCNTs. The high D/G ratio of CQD/NFL/CNT heterostructure compared to MWCNT can be attributed to the introduction of NiFe LDH nanostructures, which substantially modulated the degree of structural defects in the catalytic system favorable towards improved catalytic activity. The G’/G ratio is calculated to be 0.94 and 0.57 for MWCNTs and CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalysts, respectively, while the G’/D ratio is 0.92 and 0.51 for MWCNTs and CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalysts, respectively. Acid-treated MWCNTs were sonicated before in situ hydrothermal synthesis of NiFe LDH urchins on MWCNTs, which induces the creation of structural defects. The incorporation of NiFe LDH nanostructures significantly affects the reduction in the G’/G as well as G’/D ratios of CQD/NFL/CNT heterostructure through the synergistic interaction between NiFe LDH nanostructures, MWCNTs, and CQDs. The G′ peak intensity in CQD/NFL/CNT is reduced significantly as the ratio of CNTs in the heterostructure reduces. Also, as more surface species are introduced onto the sample (CQDs), the intensity of G’ peak gets reduced since it arises from a two-phonon process. The introduction of impurities (such as CQDs here) makes the catalytic system less ordered and the coupling effect that is required for the two-phonon process is not allowed [61]. Thus, the above results indicate the formation of strongly coupled, heterostructured CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst.

To further investigate the interactions between CQDs, CNTs, and NiFe LDH, FTIR analysis was performed. The FTIR spectra of MWCNT, NFL, NFL/CNT, and CQD/NFL/CNT are shown in Fig. 1C. In the FTIR spectra of MWCNT sample, the intense and broad IR band at ∼3416 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of isolated surface –OH moieties and/or –OH in carboxyl groups and in sorbed water. The small peak at ∼2890 cm−1 is seen, which corresponds to the –CH stretching absorption band. The IR bands of CC vibration are observed at around 1629 cm−1. The C–O stretching vibrations arising from the acid functionalization of MWCNTs is evidently captured at ∼1061 cm−1 [64]. In NFL, NFL/CNT, and CQD/NFL/CNT catalysts, the broad band at ∼3473 cm−1 is attributed to the torsional vibrations of physisorbed water (interparticle and interlayer water molecule), stretching vibrations of the structural
OH −
groups (Ni/Fe–OH), characteristic valency vibrations of hydrogen-bonded OH groups (OH⋯OH), and/or characteristic stretching vibrations of M − OH which is preserved even after compositing with MWCNTs [65]. The small peaks at ∼2890 cm−1 and 1629 cm−1 corresponding to the –CH stretching absorption and CC vibration band from MWCNTs are also preserved with reduced intensities in the composited samples. The broad and medium intensity band at ∼1356 cm−1 can be attributed to the antisymmetric stretching mode of the intercalated
CO 3
2 −
species. The prominent absorption bands below 1000 cm−1 (between 500 and 800 cm−1) belong to the metal and oxygen lattice vibrations (M − O, M-O-M, and O-M-O) in the hydrotalcite-like lattice [66]. These characteristic peaks are retained in the composited heterostructures, NFL/CNT and CQD/NFL/CNT. The peak arising from C–O stretching vibrations in MWCNTs is quenched in the composited catalysts and is barely visible compared to the other broad and high intensity bands. On the other hand, other characteristic peaks substantially maintain their intensities and positions which suggests the minimal degree of disorder even after the heterostructure formation. These results confirm the formation of hydrotalcite structure of NiFe LDH and CQD/NFL/CNT heterostructured nanocomposite.

Fig. 1D exhibits the optical reflectance properties of NFL, NFL/CNT and CQD/NFL/CNT nanocomposite using Ultraviolet–Visible Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (UV-DRS) to reveal the energy band gap. The obtained reflectance data was modified with the Kubelka-Munk function to generate Tauc plots which in turn gave the band gap of the electrocatalyst [67]. The direct band gaps deduced for MWCNT, NFL, NFL/CNT, and CQD/NFL/CNT nanocomposite are 0.72, 2.1, 2.39, and 1.42 eV, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 1D. Even though MWCNT shows the lowest bandgap among the investigated materials, the significant reduction in the band gap of CQD/NFL/CNT nanocomposite compared to the unmodified NFL indicates the presence of additional energy levels arising from strong and synergetic interactions between 0D-CQDs, 3D-NFL, and 1D-CNTs. These interactions facilitate much easier excitation of electrons to the conduction band when the material is photoexcited. During OER, the electrons transferred across the electrode-electrolyte interface as to reach the conduction band of electrocatalyst for it to be collected by the Ni form substrate. Thus, the significantly lower band gap denotes the need for lower overpotentials to transfer the electrons gained from
OH −
during OER, improving the overall OER electrocatalytic activity.

In order to study the formation of 0D-3D-1D heterostructured morphology, the CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst was characterized by Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) and High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM). Fig. S3 shows the FESEM images of the NiFe LDH powder sample. The powder samples show micron-sized fused flakes made from the more commonly observed nanoflowers of the LDH. On the other hand, growing the LDH in situ on Ni foam causes the formation of long and thin 2D nanoribbons of the LDH as shown in Fig. S4. Heterogeneous nucleation on Ni foam introduces anisotropy in the growth of LDH, resulting in the formation of long nanoribbons with reduced dispersity. The images of MWCNT-coated Ni foam are also shown in Fig. S5. Fig. 2 B shows the FESEM images of the CQD/NFL/CNT composite, which indicates that the NiFe LDH is effectively grown with good surface coverage by the in-situ hydrothermal synthesis whereas pure Ni foam exhibits a smooth surface (Fig. 2A). Fig. 2C and D depict the formation of urchin-shaped morphologies comprising 2D nanoribbons of NiFe LDH. The NiFe LDH is formed as three-dimensional urchin-like morphology with an average breadth of ∼2 μm over the CNT-coated Ni foam (Fig. 2C). The NiFe LDH urchins contain spatially arranged 2D NiFe LDH nanoribbons. Considering the images of the CQD/NFL/CNT (Fig. 2C), NFL powder sample (Fig. S3) and NFL (without CNT) grown in situ on Ni foam (Fig. S4), it is evident that this interesting urchin-like morphology is achieved only by the incorporation of CNT during the nucleation and crystal growth under hydrothermal conditions. This may be due to the CNTs acting as a shape-directing template, further enhancing the anisotropy of the nanoribbons and reducing their dispersity. The Ni/Fe ratios in different LDH samples were confirmed using Energy Dispersive Spectra (EDS) (Fig. S6). The elemental mapping depicted in Fig. 2E-H clearly confirms the uniform distribution of Ni (red), Fe (green) and O (blue) elements ascribed to NiFe LDH over the Ni foam and enough concentration of carbon (pink) indicates the presence of CQDs decorated on the surface of 3D-NFL. EDS results shown in Fig. 2I establish the 1:1 ratio (6.4/6.1) among Ni and Fe in the CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst. The increased percentage of carbon (51%) is due to the presence of both CQDs and CNTs in the CQD/NFL/CNT nanocomposite. The results of FESEM and EDS mapping clearly show that a 0D-3D-1D heterostructured CQD/NFL/CNT nanocomposite was formed.

Further, to elucidate the crystallographic information and the morphology of 0D-3D-1D CQD/NFL/CNT nanocomposite, TEM analysis was performed. Fig. 3 (A-B) show the TEM images of CQD/NFL/CNT heterostructured composite revealing the existence of 0D CQDs decorated on the surface of NiFe-LDH supported on 1D CNTs. The size of 0D-CQDs was measured to be 5 ± 2 nm and the diameter of CNT was measured as 15 ± 4 nm. The SAED depicted in Fig. 3C exhibits a ring pattern, signifying the polycrystalline nature of 3D NiFe-LDH along with 1D CNT and 0D CQDs. Each bright spotted ring observed in the SAED pattern was indexed carefully with corresponding crystal planes. The planes (0 0 6), (0 1 2), (0 1 5), (0 1 8), and (1 1 0), ascribed to 3D NiFe LDHs and the planes (0 0 6), (0 0 2), (1 0 5) and (1 0 2), corresponding to graphitic carbon of 0D-CQDs/1D-CNT also show good agreement with XRD results and standard JCPDS cards for NiFe LDH and graphitic carbon [40,58,59]. This further confirms the successful formation of heterostructured CQD/NFL/CNT nanocomposite. Moreover, Fig. 3(D-F) show the HRTEM images of 0D CQDs, 3D NiFe LDH, and 1D CNTs, respectively. The lattice fringes for CQDs, NiFe LDH, and CNTs are clearly differentiated in a single HR-TEM image shown in Fig. 3D. The d-spacing value was estimated using Digital Micrograph software using the inverse FFT image along with the profile graph. Multiple ring-like fringes are observed for 0D-CQDs with a fringe spacing of 1.8 Å (Fig. 3D) corresponding to the (0 0 2) plane of graphitic

carbon. Also, the lattice fringes of 1D CNTs confirm the existence of multi-walled in CNTs with the d-spacing of 1.7 Å, corresponding to the (0 0 4) plane of graphitic carbon which can be measured from the inverse FFT images, whose profiles are shown as insets in Fig. 3 F. Fig. 3 E clearly displays well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar distance of 1.46 Å matching the (1 1 6) crystallographic planes of NiFe LDH. Thus, the HR-TEM results clearly reveal the formation of 0D-3D-1D heterostructured CQD/NFL/CNT nanocomposite.

The surface chemical composition and elemental binding states of the prepared CQD/NFL/CNT composite were analyzed by XPS and Fig. 4
depicts the results. The survey scan spectrum (Fig. S7) confirms the presence of Ni, Fe, O, and C in the composite material. The absence of other elements in the survey scan spectrum confirms that there are no impurities in the nanocomposite. The Ni/Fe atomic ratio is found to be 2.3/1.9 which is in good agreement with the EDS results shown for CQD/NFL/CNT in Fig. 2I. As exhibited in Fig. 4A, the peaks of Ni 2p at 855.9 and 873.8 eV can be attributed to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 respectively, which correspond to the Ni2+ species. The subsidiary satellite peaks at 861.90 and 879.8 eV demonstrate the presence of Ni in the high-spin divalent state [68]. The spin-orbit splitting value between Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 is found to be 17.9 eV, which indicates that Ni exhibits 2+ state [69]. In Fig. 4B–a pair of prominent peaks of Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 at 712.3 and 724.9 eV correspond to the Fe3+ state. These two peaks are accompanied by two minor satellite peaks at ∼718.6 and ∼732.1 eV [68]. Fig. 4C depicts the high-resolution O 1s spectra having three peaks around 528.1, 530.7, and 532.7 eV. The peak at 528.1 eV indicates the lattice metal-oxygen bond. The prominent and large area peak at 530.7 eV observed from the lattice metal-OH bonds indicates the formation of hydroxides of Ni and Fe. The peak at 532.7 eV suggests the presence of adsorbed water molecules in the lattice [70]. The C 1s peaks in Fig. 4D can be fitted to four peaks corresponding to
C − C
(283.1 eV),
C = C
(284.7 eV),
C − O
(286.4 eV), and
O − C = O
(289.1 eV) which arises from the functionalization of CNT as well as the functional groups present in CQD [71].

Taken together, these results unambiguously confirm the formation of a 0D-3D-1D heterostructured CQD/NFL/CNT nanocomposite with the 0D CQDs decorated on the surface of 3D urchin-like 1:1 NFL made of 2D nanoribbons, which in turn are present on 1D MWCNT on Ni foam.

The electrochemical performance of the prepared electrocatalysts was analyzed using a conventional three-electrode system with saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode and Pt wire as the counter electrode. Polarization curves were recorded using Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) in 1 M KOH electrolyte at a scan rate of 1 mVs−1. The OER kinetics was determined by calculating the overpotentials needed for the reaction and the Tafel slopes for each electrode. Commercial benchmark catalyst RuO2 was dip coated on Ni foam and its OER kinetics was also determined under the same conditions. All the potentials were IR compensated and corrected to Reversible Hydrogen Electrode (RHE, Fig. S8).

It is known that OER on NiFe LDH in alkaline conditions follow a four-step process (Scheme 2). Each step has on electron transfer from
OH −
ions to the NiFe LDH, along with either a proton transfer or proton removal as H2O. OER electrocatalysis is due to the synergetic effect between Ni2+/Ni3+ and Fe2+/Fe3+ active sites. The observed electrocatalytic performance is crucially influenced by multiple factors include, (i) surface density and distribution of Ni2+/Ni3+ and Fe2+/Fe3+ active sites on the LDHs (atomic scale), (ii) morphology of LDHs (mesoscale) and (iii) Electrochemically Active Surface Area (macro scale). Recently Lu et al. synthesized NiFe LDH which exhibited a low overpotential of 296 mV at 100 mAcm−2 [77] while here the best NiFe LDH system catalyzes OER with 201 mV at the same current density of 100 mAcm−2. The reduction in overpotential can be attributed to the 2D nanoribbons shaped LDH obtained during the in-situ growth of NiFe LDH on Ni foam by hydrothermal synthesis. The peculiar 3D morphology increases the catalytic surface area which facilitates oxygen evolution at an increased rate [78].

Effect of Heterostructuring: For further enhancing the performance towards OER, the NiFe LDH catalyst was modified with CNT and CQD. As depicted in Fig. 5C, bare NiFe LDH, CQD decorated NiFe LDH (CQD/NFL), CNT heterostructured LDH (NFL/CNT), and the heterostructured composite CQD/NFL/CNT exhibited 201, 193, 184, and 172 mV overpotentials at 100 mAcm−2. The Tafel slopes were calculated as 46.1, 54.5, 45.6, and 37.2 mVdec−1 for the electrodes NiFe LDH, CQD/NFL, NFL/CNT, and CQD/NFL/CNT respectively. Owing to the electron withdrawing abilities, CQDs enhance the electron transfer from the electrolyte to the Ni/Fe active sites. Thus, they accelerate the M2+ to M3+ transitions and improve the OER kinetics [48,79,80]. When CQDs are incorporated with NiFe LDH the overpotential decreases from 201 mV to 193 mV at 100 mAcm−2. The OER catalytic activity of CQD/NFL catalyst is superior to the previously reported works at 100 mAcm−2 current density. Further, CNTs, being an interconnected network structure, are also good electron collectors and increase electron conductivity through tunneling [81]. Therefore, NiFe LDH (1:1) was grown in situ on MWCNT-Ni foam. This resulted in the formation of 3D urchin like morphologies of NiFe LDH comprising 2D nanoribbons. For this system, it was seen that the overpotential reduces to 184 mV from 201 mV at 100 mAcm−2. Heterostructuring with CNTs enhances the electron transfer from Ni/Fe active sites to the underlying Ni foam. Thus, increased OER activity was observed in NFL/CQD electrocatalyst. NFL/CNT and NFL catalysts show almost the same overpotentials to drive high current densities above 600 mAcm−2 while the unmodified NFL exhibits better performance than CQD/NFL at higher current densities (Fig. 5B).

Decorating NiFe LDH grown on Ni-foam with CNTs and CQD to form the 0D-3D-1D heterostructure improves electron transfer from the electrolyte (OH− ion) to the substrate (Ni foam). CQD increases the effective transport of electrons from the electrolyte to the NiFe LDH catalyst while CNT facilitates tunneling channels for effective electron transfer from the active sites to the Ni foam substrate. Heterostructuring CQD/NFL/CNT in such a rational design enables the synergetic effect between CQD, NiFe LDH, and CNT demonstrated by the lowest overpotential of 172 mV at a higher current density of 100 mAcm−2 with the low Tafel slope of 37.2 mVdec−1 superior to all other OER catalysts reported in the literature (Table 1).

Further, the correlation between overpotentials and current densities was studied for all the electrocatalysts prepared. As shown in Fig. 5D, the 0D-3D-1D heterostructured CQD/NFL/CNT catalyst exhibited exceptional capability to increase the current density with a minimal increase in overpotential. To achieve a high current density of 950 mAcm−2 from 50 mAcm−2, CQD/NFL/CNT only needed a minimal 50 mV increase in the overpotential. Further, the overpotentials remained remarkably constant across a wide range of current densities from 500 to 950 mAcm−2. Specifically, the electrocatalyst showed low overpotentials of 184 mV, 204 mV and 212 mV at 200 mAcm−2, 500 mAcm−2, 750 mAcm−2, respectively. The excellent synergy established between CQDs, CNTs, and NFL and the rational 0D-3D-1D heterostructure eliminates the need for an increase in the overpotential as the operating current densities increase. This is a particularly interesting result for practical utilization of the CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst at higher operating current densities.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy: EIS analysis was carried out at a constant overpotential of 200 mV to understand the charge transfer capabilities and the reaction kinetics of the electrocatalysts. The Nyquist plots of the four electrodes NFL, CQD/NFL, NFL/CNT and CQD/NFL/CNT, and the equivalent circuit model (in the inset) are displayed in Fig. S11. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) values were deduced by fitting the obtained impedance spectra. The CQD/NFL/CNT heterostructure has an Rct of 206.2 mΩ which is lower than that of CQD/NFL (257.3 mΩ), NFL/CNT (235.5 mΩ), and NFL (336.8 mΩ). The Rct values of NFL, CQD/NFL, and NFL/CNT are much lower compared to the earlier reported ones (Table 1). The lower Rct values indicate the faster electron transport at the electrocatalyst interface and high intrinsic electron conductivity, which also correlates with the low Tafel slope values [82]. This can be ascribed to the 0D-3D-1D heterostructured nanoarchitecture design of CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst in which CQDs facilitate faster electron transfer at the electrolyte-NiFe LDH interface and CNT networks boost the electron transport from NiFe LDH to the underlying Ni foam substrate.

Durability and Stability: Long-term durability and stability of the prepared 0D-3D-1D heterostructured CQD/NFL/CNT catalyst were evaluated using chronopotentiometry studies. Commercial RuO2 catalyst-coated Ni foam, and bare Ni foam were also subjected to chronopotentiometry analysis for comparison, and the graphs are shown in Fig. 5E. The analysis was conducted at a constant 100 mAcm−2 current density for 100 h. The hierarchical 0D-3D-1D nanostructured composite CQD/NFL/CNT shows exceptional stability at the high current density of 100 mAcm−2 at a low potential of 1.44 V for 100 h with a very minimum drift of 7 mV from the initial potential, which is exceptional compared to previously reported works [32,63]. Bare Ni foam was highly unstable even before 10 h of continuous chronopotentiometry. After 100 h, the potential drift was about 330 mV for bare Ni foam while commercial RuO2 catalyst exhibited a 35 mV drift to reach a potential of 1.59 V for 100 mAcm−2 current density after 100 h.

LSV and EIS analyses were performed after the long-term chronopotentiometry study for the CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst to understand the changes that the catalyst may have undergone during the stability test. From the EIS spectra in the inset of Fig. 5F, it is observed that the charge transfer resistance of the material increases to 193.6 mΩ, an increase of only 12.6 mΩ from the initial Rct of 206.2 mΩ. The LSV polarization curves recorded after the chronopotentiometry analysis also show slight increase in the potentials. The overpotentials needed to drive 100, 200 and 300 mAcm−2 current densities shifted minimally by 8, 10 and 13 mV, respectively. These results depict that the performance of the 0D-3D-1D nanoarchitecture CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst remained nearly the same even after a long-term, continuous operation of 100 h. Importantly, even after the long-term durability test, the same catalyst material is capable of driving similar higher current densities as before. This superior stability can be attributed to the rational design of heterostructured 0D-3D-1D nanoarchitecture of the CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst.

Electrochemically Active Surface Area (ECSA): To understand further about the reason behind the enhanced performance, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of each material was examined and compared. ECSA was quantified using electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) which was calculated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis in the non-Faradaic region. CVs were analyzed at different scan rates varying from 10 to 80 mVs−1 in the non-Faradaic region for the four materials (Fig. 6 A-D). Cdl values for each electrode were calculated from the linear slope of the capacitive current density vs scan rate plot (Fig. S12). As observed in Fig. 6E, the Cdl value of CQD/NFL/CNT is found to be 5.56 mF/cm2, which is 2 times higher than the double layer capacitance of CQD/NFL and 1.5 times greater than that of NFL and NFL/CNT. The very high Cdl value observed for CQD/NFL/CNT indicates the higher catalytic surface area of the catalyst and in agreement with its higher OER catalytic activity.

Fig. 6F compares the ECSA calculated for each electrode. As seen, the composite CQD/NFL/CNT has a large ECSA, calculated to be 252.72 cm2. A larger ECSA value translates to a greater number of active sites and better catalytic activity. The increased Cdl and ECSA can be explained by the introduction of CQDs and CNTs and the urchin like 3D morphologies grown on 1D MWCNTs, which enhanced the number of active sites in the 0D-3D-1D nanoarchitecture of the CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst. This is evident from the comparable and almost equal ECSA and Cdl values of the three prepared CQD/NFL, NFL and NFL/CNT catalysts, and a significant jump in the ECSA in CQD/NFL/CNT heterostructure. The bare NFL catalyst actually has a slightly higher ECSA and Cdl than that of CQD/NFL electrocatalyst. This may be because of the increased dispersion of CQDs on the Ni and Fe active sites, blocking the active surface area and the real active sites. However, the presence of CQDs increases the electron transport and electrical conductivity at the electrolyte-NiFe LDH interface and the OER overpotential was still minimized for CQD/NFL than unmodified NFL. The enlarged surface area and the additional active sites enhances the OER catalytic activity of the CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst and makes it superior to the rest of the systems. The real activity in catalytic sites was further examined by normalizing the OER current densities by ECSA. The ECSA-normalized LSV polarization curves are shown in Fig. S13. The LSV curves exhibited the same trends as in the former curves without ECSA normalization. CQD/NFL/CNT composite, being the catalyst with higher ECSA, still exhibited higher performance than all the other catalytic systems even after normalization with its large ECSA. Thus, the 0D-3D-1D heterostructured CQD/NFL/CNT is a high-performing electrocatalyst for OER with very low overpotentials and exceptional stability even at high current densities for long hours.

Mechanism: The aforementioned results confirmed that the modification of CQDs, in conjunction with CNTs support, can enhance the electrocatalytic activities and stability of NiFe LDH. This is due to the bonding and electronic interactions that occur between the 1D-CNTs, 0D-CQDs, and the 3D-NiFe LDH. Therefore, from the obtained results and discussion, the synergetic effect on the OER electrocatalytic activity of 0D-3D-1D heterostructured CQD/NFL/CNT nanocomposite and a plausible oxidation mechanism is presented in Scheme 2.

Typically, NiFe layered double hydroxides are brucite-like structures in which trivalent metal cations are partially replaced in the position of octahedrally ordered divalent metal cations, which can be depicted as
Ni
1 − x
2 +
Fe x
3 +
( OH )
2
[23,109,110]. Tuning the molar ratio of metal cations Ni2+ and Fe3+ in the LDH system growing insitu on MWCNT coated nickel foam, and decorating it with CQDs resulted in 0D-3D-1D nanoarchitectures with significant physio-chemical and electrochemical properties. During the OH− oxidation process, both Ni2+ and Fe3+ undergo redox reactions (Scheme 2B). Electrons can transfer through both Ni2+/Ni3+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ via successive
adsorption of OH− and desorption of H2O and O2. Oxygen evolution occurs through a consecutive

four-electron transfer process at the Ni2+/Ni3+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ active sites. Initially, OH− ion adsorbs on the surface of active sites by a single electron oxidation process giving M-OH∗ (M = Ni/Fe) species. Even though the metal ion M2+ is already part of a hydroxide, this adsorption of an additional OH− is facilitated through a cumulative effect of interactions of the M2+/M3+ sites with neighboring metal ions, the ionically bonded OH− ions and the intercalating Cl− and
CO 3
2 −
anions. Both CQD and CNT then further facilitate this adsorption through their electron withdrawing capabilities. The as-formed M-OH∗ and another hydroxyl (OH−) ion go through proton transfer (removal of H2O) to generate M-O∗ as in the second step. Then, the M-O∗ and another hydroxyl (OH−) ion combine to form an intermediate M-OOH∗ in the third step. Finally, the O2 evolves from the M-OOH∗ via an electron transfer step leaving the metal active site free for consecutive reactions [25,41,111,112]. These steps are depicted below:
(1)
M +
OH −
→ M −
OH ∗
+
e −
(2)
M −
OH ∗
+
OH −
→ M −
O ∗
+
H 2
O
( l )
+
e −
(3)
M −
O ∗
+
OH −
→ M −
OOH ∗
+
e −
(4)
M −

From the above mechanism, it is evident that the primary electrocatalytic performance should be ascribed to the Ni/Fe active sites in the LDH. The optimal Ni/Fe ratio of the LDH results in the most facile reversible electron transfer i.e., M2+ to M3+to M2+ as in the reaction mechanism. The transition from M-OH∗ species to M-OOH∗ through M-O∗ affects the rate of water oxidation [73]. When the Ni/Fe ratio is 1:1, this transition becomes more feasible due to the synergy between Fe and Ni active sites [111,113,114]. Further, the improvement of the oxidation process on NiFe LDH has been established when the LDH is modified with CQDs and CNTs. The randomly decorated CQDs on NiFe LDH surface will act as an electron sink owing to 0D quantum confinement. Since CQDs exhibit high surface area with excellent conductivity, CQDs could promote easy and fast charge transfer from the OH− ions in the electrolyte to the Ni and Fe active sites in the LDH thereby facilitating the OER [80,115]. Moreover, the carboxyl CO group associated with the CQDs tends to bind with the NiFe LDH to form C–O–Ni/C–O–Fe as covalent bonds [79,116] (as evident from FTIR and XPS results in Figs. 1C and 4D, respectively). Along with strong electrostatic interactions, these enable electron transfer across CQDs and NiFe LDH in the nanocomposite [48]. Furthermore, these CQD/NiFe LDH when supported with 1D MWCNTs could enable a rapid electron transfer from the CQDs/NiFe LDH site to the underlying Ni foam through electron tunneling in the MWCNT, which facilitates an exceptional OER performance along with long-term stability. Along with greater electrical conductivity, CNTs also improve the faster electron transfer kinetics [81,117,118]. This in turn accelerates the rate of M2+- M3+-M2+ reversible transition.

Therefore, as a composite, CNTs underlying the NiFe LDH nanosheets encourage effective electron collection from the Ni/Fe LDH active sites to the Ni foam beneath through tunneling and CQDs on the top of NiFe LDHs enhance the electron transfer from the electrolyte to the NiFe LDH active site. Such fast electron transfer kinetics has been achieved in a highly stable manner in alkaline media because of the novel 0D-3D-1D heterostructured arrangement of CQD/NFL/CNT electrocatalyst.

In summary, this work presents an engineered hierarchical structure of NiFe LDH with: (a) modulated composition and coordination environment and (b) a unique 0D-3D-1D heterostructure with 0D CQDs, 3D LDH urchins and 1D MWCNTs, that promotes effective electron transfer for OER from OH− ions in the electrolyte to the LDH electrocatalyst and subsequently to the underlying Ni foam electrode. This heterostructure has a significantly high electrochemically active surface area (ECSA), 252.72 cm2 on a 1 × 1 cm2 Ni foam surface. As a result, the 0D-3D-1D nanostructured hierarchical CQD/NFL/CNT catalytic system exhibited exceptional OER catalytic performance with a very low overpotential of 172 mV at a higher current density of 100 mAcm−2, a small Tafel slope of 37.2 mVdec−1, and excellent stability at 1.44 V over 100 h chronopotentiometric test at a constant current density of 100 mAcm−2, significantly better to the commercial OER catalysts and most of the reported electrocatalysts so far. Further, the electrocatalyst shows remarkably low overpotentials of merely 184 mV at 200 mAcm−2, 204 mV at 500 mAcm−2, and 212 mV at 750 mAcm−2, respectively. This novel 0D-3D-1D nanostructured hierarchical design strategy provides new insights into development of high-performance, low-cost, durable, LDH-based OER electrocatalysts for alkaline water electrolysis.
